cves.description:
evernote-mcp-server openBrowser Command Injection Privilege Escalation Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows local attackers to escalate privileges on affected installations of evernote-mcp-server. An attacker must first obtain the ability to execute low-privileged code on the target system in order to exploit this vulnerability.

The specific flaw exists within the openBrowser function. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of a user-supplied string before using it to execute

cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function sort_cmp of the file src/array.c. Such manipulation leads to use after free. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The name of the patch is eb398971bfb43c38db3e04528b68ac9a7ce509bc. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: AI-assisted code in cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390 rewrote sort_cmp and left only a pointer-equality modification check (`RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`), removing the earlier size-based guard and directly writing the lines later fixed by eb398971. The original stale-pointer design came from an earlier human commit, but the AI-assisted reimplementation/regression materially contributed to the vulnerable state that shipped.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: array: implement hybrid sorting with algorithm selection

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-assisted commit rewrote `sort_cmp` to take element values instead of indices, added `insertion_sort`, and replaced the earlier post-callback guard with a pointer-only check: `if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p)`. The fix commit eb398971 changes exactly this logic by snapshotting both pointer and length and checking `RARRAY_LEN(ary) != n`, so cf8faed directly authored the insufficient check that the patch corrects. The screening hypothesis is directionally correct: this commit did not just move code; it removed the prior length/index-based safeguard and left the comparison path vulnerable to in-place shrink/realloc that preserves the same pointer.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: kernel.c: remove mrb_inspect_recursive_p(); #5531

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Its `src/array.c` diff only changes `mrb_ary_to_s` from `mrb_inspect_recursive_p()` to `MRB_RECURSIVE_UNARY_P(...)`. It does not touch `sort_cmp`, `heapify`, `insertion_sort`, or `mrb_ary_sort_bang`, so it has no causal connection to the use-after-free in sorting.

The original stale-pointer design came from an earlier human commit, but the AI-assisted reimplementation/regression materially contributed to the vulnerable state that shipped.

cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-coauthored commit 2b72d8a7c153e2afb22245ad9e40e0c7d5b1aa70 directly introduced the unsafe JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization by writing `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` without verifying that the preceding instruction was actually `OP_JMPNOT`. AI-coauthored commit 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0 also helped create the exploit path by making undefined pinned variables compile to a 3-byte unconditional `OP_JMP`, the exact non-JMPNOT case called out by the fix message.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the exact vulnerable optimization that the fix changes. Its diff added the `if ((int32_t)(fail_pos + 2) + ... && fail_pos + 2 == s->pc) { s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF; }` block, and pickaxe searches for both `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` and `fail_pos + 2 == s->pc` point to this commit as the origin. The later fix e50f15 adds the missing `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT` check, confirming that 2b72's assumption about the instruction type was the root bug.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize pattern matching for array literals

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit did add another way for `fail_pos` to come from a 3-byte `OP_JMP` (`tmp = genjmp(s, OP_JMP, *fail_pos)` in the known-array-length mismatch fast path), so it broadened exposure to the same bad assumption. But it did not introduce the unsafe optimization itself, and the project was already vulnerable after 2b72 because the earlier pin-operator code from 1de634 could already produce the same non-`OP_JMPNOT` fail link. So bbd5 is connected contextually, but it is not the commit that introduced the CVE fixed by e50f15.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: add pin operator to pattern matching

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit added `NODE_PAT_PIN` and, for an undefined pinned variable, compiled the failure path as `tmp = genjmp(s, OP_JMP, *fail_pos); *fail_pos = tmp;` instead of rejecting the construct. The fix message for e50f15 explicitly cites this exact scenario: an undefined pinned variable produces a 3-byte `OP_JMP`, so `fail_pos - 2` does not point to an `OP_JMPNOT` opcode. Although 1de634 did not itself overwrite bytecode, it introduced the unsafe fail-link shape and missing validation that made 2b72's new optimization exploitable.

cves.ai_contribution: Yes. AI-coauthored commit 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0 also helped create the exploit path by making undefined pinned variables compile to a 3-byte unconditional `OP_JMP`, the exact non-JMPNOT case called out by the fix message.

cves.description: Titra API Contains Mass Assignment Vulnerability
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The original unsafe `customfields` design in task/timecard methods was human-authored, but the AI-authored Copilot commit `7a4aa852650b69d40e89fca09c7ad9fb0ad4ce7b` added the `/project/task/create/` API endpoint that directly merged `...json.customfields` after trusted task fields, creating an additional exploitable mass-assignment path. AI therefore materially contributed to the CVE even though it was not the sole or earliest source of the flaw.

bug_commits.author: copilot-swe-agent[bot]
bug_commits.message: Add estimatedHours field to tasks and implement task management API endpoints

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit added the `/project/task/create/` REST endpoint in `server/APIroutes.js` and directly inserted `{ projectId, name, start, end, estimatedHours, dependencies, ...json.customfields }`. Pickaxe shows `...json.customfields,` first appears in this file in `7a4aa852`, so this is the origin of that vulnerable API path. Because attacker-controlled `customfields` can override protected task fields, this commit is a confirmed mass-assignment introducer.

cves.description: eBay API MCP Server Affected by Environment Variable Injection
cves.how_introduced: Yes. Claude-authored commit 4c9c826c6fc8b64a20e948cb46fefdda42d5244d introduced the unsafe `updateEnvFile()` helper and wired it into token-setting paths, causing caller-controlled token strings to be written directly into `.env` via string interpolation. Claude also co-authored 8c1989e36ad2950fcf4c8f9f95027ebf487e0f61, which rewrote the same sink to add quotes but still left values unescaped, so AI-authored/AI-assisted code was part of the causal chain.

bug_commits.author: Claude
bug_commits.message: feat: Auto-update .env file with refreshed tokens

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first introduction of `updateEnvFile()` (`git log -S 'function updateEnvFile'` points to 4c9c826c) and it wrote the core vulnerable pattern: regex-based text replacement plus direct interpolation of token values into `.env` (`const newLine = `${key}=${value}``). Blame at the fixed file shows 4c9c826c also introduced the calls from `setUserTokens()`, `getOrRefreshAppAccessToken()`, and `refreshUserToken()`, so attacker-controlled or externally supplied token strings started being persisted through this unsafe sink in this commit. The fix removes this entire approach and replaces it with `dotenv.parse` + `dotenv-stringify`, confirming 4c9c826c as the original introducer.

bug_commits.author: George Peden
bug_commits.message: Fix: quote token values in .env to prevent # truncation

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit changed the critical line from `${key}=${value}` to `${key}="${value}"`, and blame shows it authored the exact interpolation line removed by the fix. Although the helper was already unsafe before, this commit re-authored the sink in a still-vulnerable form: it continued concatenating unescaped token data into `.env`, now allowing quote-breaking payloads instead of addressing the underlying injection problem. Under the 'reimplementation is bug introduction' rule, this is a confirmed BIC for the vulnerable pre-fix state.

bug_commits.author: YosefHayim
bug_commits.message: refactor: migrate to .env-only token management, remove file-based persistence

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This earlier refactor migrated token management to `.env`-only configuration, but it did not add any code that wrote untrusted values back into `.env`; `setUserTokens()` remained memory-only and refresh logic merely instructed the user to update `.env` manually. The security fix did not reverse the `.env`-based design, only the unsafe write implementation added later. So this commit is architectural background, not the commit that introduced the injection sink.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-assisted commits authored the original bucket schema that stored all of an entityŌĆÖs buckets on a single DynamoDB partition key, and later reimplemented that same design in the composite-bucket rewrite. Those AI-authored commits are the root cause of the hot-partition DoS condition; the later Feb. 21 sharding commits were mitigation work, not the original introducers.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodr├®
bug_commits.message: Ō£© feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Screening flagged this commit because it edits the core sharding logic, but the exact changes here are mitigation work: random shard selection, shard retry, and `bump_shard_count()` after the system already moved toward pre-sharded buckets. The hot-partition vulnerability already existed in the older entity-key bucket design. Although later follow-up commit `e67d011...` refines this logic, this commit does not introduce the original single-partition weakness described by the CVE.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodr├®
bug_commits.message: ­¤ōØ docs: update docstrings and docs site for pre-shard buckets

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is a docs/docstring update. In code, it only expands comments and docstrings around pre-shard helpers; it does not change bucket keys, sharding behavior, or DynamoDB write distribution. The blame hits are from documentation churn in the same files, not from vulnerability-introducing logic.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodr├®
bug_commits.message: Initial commit: zae-limiter rate limiting library

cves.description: Heap-buffer-overflow in tinyobj::tryParseDouble
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-authored commit f27721f3c9cd20af4d4eee789431b92d0e0c4477 rewrote parsing to use exact-length StreamReader buffers and passed raw buffer slices (`start`, `start + len`) into the legacy `tryParseDouble`, making its pre-existing out-of-bounds read reachable on malformed EOF-terminated numbers. cves.description: Coolify members can see private key of root user
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code (Claude Code) directly contributed to the vulnerability in multiple ways: (1) f8146f5a59 added a debug toggle feature to deployment views that exposed debug logs (potentially containing private keys) to all team members without any role-based access check; (2) f152ec00ad deliberately weakened an authorization check from 'update' to 'view' permission for loadStorageOnServer(), allowing read-only members to access server file contents; (3) b878dc8102 introduced an API middleware authorization check with a flawed `(int)` cast on team_id that could be bypassed when team_id was null/0; (4) f77ad4cbd9 migrated Storage/Form.php to use explicit public properties for `$key` and `$secret` without adding member access controls.

bug_commits.author: Andras Bacsai
bug_commits.message: refactor(auth): enforce team member authorization across app

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the API middleware authorization check with a bug: it used `$teamId = (int) data_get($token, 'team_id')` which casts null to 0, and then `if ($teamId && ...)` evaluates to false when teamId is 0 (falsy), completely bypassing the member restriction check. The fix changes to `$teamId = data_get($token, 'team_id')` (no cast) and `if ($teamId !== null && ...)` to properly handle the null case. This commit wrote the flawed authorization logic that the fix corrects. While this commit was trying to ADD security controls, the implementation was buggy and could be bypassed.

bug_commits.author: Andras Bacsai
bug_commits.message: Add log search, download, and collapsible sections with lazy loading

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-authored commit (Claude Code) added the `$is_debug_enabled` property and `toggleDebug()` method to the Deployment Show component. Before this commit, there was no debug toggle feature in this component. The fix modifies exactly the line `$this->is_debug_enabled = $this->application->settings->is_debug_enabled;` to gate it behind `auth()->user()->isMember()` check, and the Blade template wraps the toggle button in `@can('update', $application)`. This commit introduced the vulnerable code pattern: exposing debug information to all authenticated users without role-based access control.

bug_commits.author: Andras Bacsai
bug_commits.message: fix: Detect read-only Docker volumes with long-form syntax and enable refresh

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-authored commit (Claude Code) explicitly changed the authorization check in `loadStorageOnServer()` from `$this->authorize('update', $this->resource)` to `$this->authorize('view', $this->resource)`, with a comment stating 'Loading content is a read operation, so we use view permission'. The fix reverses this exact change back to 'update' permission. Before this commit, the authorization required 'update' permission, which was more restrictive. This deliberate weakening of the permission check allowed team members with only 'view' access to load server file contents that could contain sensitive data.

bug_commits.message: fix: prevent TypeError in database General components with null server

The fix adds `isPasswordHiddenForMember` logic to hide passwords from members, which is added AFTER the mount() method's existing code. The password properties (`$clickhouseAdminPassword`) were introduced earlier by commit 7fb1a1fc75 (non-AI, 2024). This commit didn't introduce or modify any code related to password visibility or member access control; it only dealt with null safety.

bug_commits.pr_title: Complete Livewire legacy model binding migration (25+ components)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-authored commit (Claude Code) migrated Storage/Form.php from Livewire legacy model binding to explicit public properties. It introduced `public string $key;` and `public string $secret;` as explicit component properties, and created the `syncData()` method that copies `$this->storage->key` and `$this->storage->secret` into these public properties. Before this commit, the storage model's key/secret were accessed through Livewire's model binding (`storage.key`, `storage.secret`), which is a different access pattern. The fix adds member-based hiding logic (`$this->key = ''; $this->secret = '';` for members) to these exact properties introduced by this commit.

cves.description:
This is an uncontrolled resource consumption vulnerability (CWE-400) that can lead to Denial of Service (DoS).

In vulnerable Undici versions, when┬Āinterceptors.deduplicate()┬Āis enabled, response data for deduplicated requests could be accumulated in memory for downstream handlers. An attacker-controlled or untrusted upstream endpoint can exploit this with large/chunked responses and concurrent identical requests, causing high memory usage and potential OOM process termination.

Impacted users a
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The AI-attributed deduplicate feature commit `b902551e2a154fac2b3923d948c074a5640c01b0` (screening links it to Claude Code subcommit `92cef77edb4c`) introduced the `DeduplicationHandler`/`deduplicate` implementation that copied every response chunk into an in-memory array and replayed the full body with `Buffer.concat`, which is exactly the design reversed by the fix. The other AI-tagged candidate, `b12081c340b69716c75a9f73687748b46858fbda`, was unrelated SOCKS5 error-type work and did not contribute.

bug_commits.author: Matteo Collina
bug_commits.message: refactor: extract request deduplication to standalone interceptor

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced `lib/handler/deduplication-handler.js` and `lib/interceptor/deduplicate.js` from scratch. In that new handler, `onResponseData()` unconditionally did `this.#chunks.push(Buffer.from(chunk))` and `#notifyWaitingHandlers()` later did `const body = Buffer.concat(this.#chunks)`, meaning deduplicated responses were fully buffered in memory before replay; `git log -S` for those exact strings points back to this commit as their first appearance. The fix `5890c7b7` removes that whole-body buffering design, streams chunks to waiting handlers, stops attaching new waiters once body streaming has begun, and adds `maxBufferSize`, so `b902551e2` is the direct bug-introducing commit for all blame hits in `lib/handler/deduplication-handler.js`, `lib/interceptor/deduplicate.js`, and the related `types/interceptors.d.ts` API addition.

cves.description: Babylon Incorrect FP inactive accounting in costaking creates ŌĆ£phantom stakeŌĆØ that earns rewards after BTC unbond
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The first commit that introduced the buggy costaking unbonding logic is 2bd9585607bd7b1c0905d21b92bcdb6d4c03bf36, an AI-attributed backport commit with a Copilot co-author trailer, and that commit directly added the flawed `if !isFpActiveInPrevSet || !isFpActiveInCurrSet` guard that leaves phantom stake behind when a finality provider becomes inactive during BTC unbonding.

bug_commits.author: RafilxTenfen
bug_commits.message: feat: costaking (#1707)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix changes `x/costaking/keeper/hooks_finality.go` so `AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded` returns early only when the FP was not active in the previous set; before the fix it returned early whenever the FP was inactive in the current set too. Pickaxe shows 2bd95856 is the first commit to add both `AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded` and the exact vulnerable conditional `if !isFpActiveInPrevSet || !isFpActiveInCurrSet`, and blame on the vulnerable lines points to this same commit. The same commit also had the now-reverted rationale about avoiding 'double subtraction', but `x/finality/keeper/power_dist_change.go` already processed BTC-delegation hooks before FP-removal hooks and `x/incentive/keeper/reward_tracker_store.go` already made `IterateBTCDelegationSatsUpdated` account for pending events, so the FP-removal hook would subtract only the updated remaining active sats. As a result, when an unbond causes an FP to leave the active set, the unbonded sats are never subtracted from the costaker tracker, creating the phantom stake fixed later.

cves.description: An HTTP request smuggling vulnerability (CWE-444) was found in Pingora's handling of HTTP/1.1 connection upgrades. The issue occurs when a Pingora proxy reads a request containing an Upgrade header, causing the proxy to pass through the rest of the bytes on the connection to a backend before the backend has accepted the upgrade. An attacker can thus directly forward a malicious payload after a request with an Upgrade header to that backend in a way that may be interpreted as a subsequent request

cves.description:
HTTP::Session2 versions before 1.12 for Perl for Perl may generate weak session ids using the rand() function.

The HTTP::Session2 session id generator returns a SHA-1 hash seeded with the built-in rand function, the epoch time, and the PID. The PID will come from a small set of numbers, and the epoch time may be guessed, if it is not leaked from the HTTP Date header. The built-in rand() function is unsuitable for cryptographic usage.

HTTP::Session2 after version 1.02 will attempt to use the /d
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI co-authored commit f8a08a8ca77a5ed61af1e2d24078ff62cc2685b3, which is the first commit in this repo to add lib/HTTP/Session2/Random.pm with the weak rand()-based fallback and the session-store modules that call it. The later AI-flagged candidate commits were false positives: they changed docs/versioning or restored callers only after Random.pm had already been fixed.

bug_commits.message: Service Accounts API (#1699)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This squash merge is the mainline commit that introduced service accounts: it adds the `/api/service-accounts` endpoints, `ServiceAccountsManager.createServiceAccount()`, and the `service` user type. The code creates a normal user identity plus API key for the service account (`getUserByLogin(..., 'service')` and `createApiKey(...)`), and the commit message explicitly says a service account is 'just a user account' that can 'act exactly like a user account.' Existing websocket/doc code does not distinguish service accounts: `DocWorker` exposes `fetchURL` to any viewer over websocket, while `DocManager.openDoc()`/`AuthSession` gate on ordinary document access rather than user type. That is the flawed design this CVE depends on.

cves.description: The openml/openml.org web application version v2.0.20241110 uses incremental user IDs and insufficient email ownership verification during email update workflows. An authenticated attacker controlling a user account with a lower user ID can update their email address to that of another user with a higher user ID without proper verification. This results in the victim's email being reassigned to the attacker's account, causing the victim to be locked out immediately and unable to log in. The vuln
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The AI-assisted commit 142f468495451755fa54f461aad01106ca281e1e reimplemented profile email changes as a direct database update with no ownership proof and, in the same change set, added email-based account lookup (`WHERE email = ? OR username = ?`) that selects the first duplicate row. Although the same design flaw originally existed in legacy Flask code, the AI-authored Next.js code recreated and materially contributed to the lower-ID/higher-ID email-takeover lockout path.

cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-authored code directly introduced the vulnerable unbounded `DecodeStream.ensureBuffer()` implementation into the monorepo in `e4a4c300cd20dc34166e25565908d0a9afdb58f5`, and Devin also co-authored `3aacf2a9b1883b160db068863310f59940681d82`, which exposed the same parser through additional user-supplied PDF manipulation APIs. The original product-level exposure started earlier in a human commit, but AI code was still part of the causal chain and wrote code the fix later changed.

bug_commits.author: Devin AI
bug_commits.message: Migrate pdf-lib into pdfme monorepo

agent_verdicts.reasoning: `e4a4c300` added `packages/pdf-lib/src/core/streams/DecodeStream.ts` to the monorepo, including the vulnerable `ensureBuffer(requested)` implementation with unbounded growth: `while (size < requested) { size *= 2; }` followed by `new Uint8Array(size)`, with no decoded-size cap. The fix commit `8c3b6a713b3de767e1bdcc37ce831ecbce0212f3` directly modifies those lines to add `MAX_DECODED_SIZE` checks, and blame on the fixed file attributes the pre-fix vulnerable lines to `e4a4c300`. Even though this was a migration/import of existing logic, reintroducing that vulnerable implementation into repo code is bug introduction under the stated criteria.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Docker container escape via unvalidated bind mount config injection
cves.signal_note: Detected claude_code (co_author_trailer signal on sub-commit 3452b61ff25f), but that sub-commit did not modify the vulnerable file. Tool inferred from the same PR ŌĆö see ai_contribution for the investigator's causal analysis.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The original unsafe bind-mount feature was introduced by a human commit, but AI-authored code contributed later by re-enabling `docker.binds` in the validated config schema inside candidate commit `eaace34233fdf454c526d23cd2fd49de3be8eb32` (screened culprit sub-commit `3452b61ff25f...`). That change made the existing unvalidated `-v` bind-mount path reachable again from user config, which is part of the causal chain for this container-escape CVE.

bug_commits.author: Aaron Konyer
bug_commits.message: fix(sandbox): restore docker.binds and preserve PATH in exec

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit added `binds: z.array(z.string()).optional()` back to the sandbox Docker Zod schema and added tests that explicitly accepted dangerous mounts such as `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`. The fix later hardens exactly this attack surface by validating sandbox Docker config before container creation and by auditing dangerous binds. Even though the `-v` sink already existed elsewhere, this commit changed the calling context by making unvalidated bind mounts user-configurable again, so it is a confirmed introducer/enabler.
